name: Power Clean
technical_cues: Start with bar over mid-foot, hip-width stance
Drive legs aggressively through the floor on the first pull
Shrug violently and pull elbows high at hip contact
Drop under the bar fast and catch in a quarter-squat
Rack the bar across the front deltoids, elbows high
biomechanical_why: The power clean is driven primarily by the gluteus maximus (origin: posterior ilium and sacrum; insertion: gluteal tuberosity of femur) and quadriceps group during the initial pull and explosive second pull phase. The trapezius (origin: occipital protuberance and spinous processes C7–T12; insertion: clavicle, acromion, and scapular spine) fires violently during the shrug to elevate the bar and create upward momentum. The hip hinge mechanics maximize posterior chain stretch-shortening cycle efficiency, allowing peak power output through triple extension of the ankle, knee, and hip simultaneously.

name: Hang Power Clean
technical_cues: Set the bar at mid-thigh with hips hinged back
Keep lats engaged to prevent the bar from swinging forward
Explode hips forward and shrug simultaneously
Catch the bar high with elbows punching forward rapidly
Absorb the catch by allowing slight knee bend
biomechanical_why: The hang position pre-loads the hamstrings (biceps femoris, semimembranosus, semitendinosus; origin: ischial tuberosity; insertion: proximal tibia and fibula) in an isometric stretch, enabling a more powerful concentric contraction during the explosive pull. The deltoids and rotator cuff muscles work eccentrically to decelerate the bar and guide it into the rack position across the anterior deltoids. Starting from the hang eliminates the first pull complexity, allowing focus on developing rate of force development through the second pull and transition.

name: Power Snatch
technical_cues: Grip wide enough so bar sits at hip crease when arms extended
Keep the bar close to the body throughout the entire pull
Achieve full triple extension before pulling under
Punch hands overhead aggressively as you drop under
Lock out wrists and stabilize the bar directly overhead
biomechanical_why: The power snatch demands the greatest combined power output of any barbell lift, relying on the posterior chain—primarily gluteus maximus and erector spinae (iliocostalis, longissimus, spinalis; origin: sacrum and posterior ilium; insertion: ribs and transverse processes)—to accelerate the bar to maximal velocity. The wide grip shifts the mechanical advantage so that triple extension projects the bar higher and allows a shallower receiving position compared to the full squat snatch. The rotator cuff (supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, subscapularis) provides critical stabilization at the glenohumeral joint once the bar is locked out overhead, preventing unwanted rotation or collapse.

name: Hang Power Snatch
technical_cues: Begin with a wide snatch grip, bar hanging at mid-thigh
Maintain a neutral spine with shoulders packed down
Drive hips explosively and shrug to initiate upward bar path
Pull elbows high and wide before punching overhead
Receive the bar with arms locked and feet flat
biomechanical_why: Initiating from the hang eliminates the first pull and forces the athlete to generate all bar velocity from the second pull, maximizing training stimulus on hip extension power through the gluteus maximus and hamstring complex. The wide snatch grip externally rotates the humerus (via infraspinatus and teres minor; origin: posterior scapula; insertion: greater tubercle of humerus), which is essential for maintaining the overhead position safely. The trapezius and serratus anterior (origin: ribs 1–8; insertion: medial border of scapula) work in concert to upwardly rotate the scapula, creating proper shoulder positioning during the overhead catch.

name: Clean and Jerk
technical_cues: Execute the clean to the front rack before pausing to reset
Dip the knees vertically 2–3 inches for the jerk drive
Drive the bar overhead explosively with legs and hips
Split the feet front-to-back aggressively during the jerk
Lock both arms out and stand up by moving feet together
biomechanical_why: The clean and jerk is a two-phase lift where the clean targets the posterior chain and the jerk targets pressing power, making it the most technically complex and demanding barbell movement. During the jerk, the quadriceps (rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, vastus intermedius; origin: anterior ilium and femoral shaft; insertion: tibial tuberosity via patellar tendon) generate the initial drive, while the triceps brachii (origin: infraglenoid tubercle and posterior humerus; insertion: olecranon process) lock out the elbows overhead. The split stance of the split jerk dramatically lowers the center of mass, reducing the height the bar must travel and allowing heavier loads to be successfully received overhead.

name: Split Jerk
technical_cues: Dip straight down with vertical torso before the drive
Drive the bar with maximum leg power, not arm press
Simultaneously split feet front-to-back as bar travels up
Lock out the front knee and punch both arms fully
Recover by stepping the front foot back first
biomechanical_why: The split jerk leverages the asymmetric lunge position to receive maximal loads overhead by lowering the body's center of mass while the bar is still rising, rather than pressing the bar to full height under load. The gluteus medius (origin: outer ilium; insertion: greater trochanter) and hip flexors (iliopsoas; origin: T12-L5 vertebrae and iliac fossa; insertion: lesser trochanter) of the front and rear legs stabilize the pelvis in the split stance and prevent lateral collapse. The serratus anterior and lower trapezius actively depress and upwardly rotate the scapula to maintain a stable overhead base and protect the acromioclavicular joint under heavy loading.

name: Push Jerk
technical_cues: Grip the bar in front rack with elbows slightly forward
Dip 2–3 inches then drive explosively through both legs
Push the bar overhead while simultaneously pressing under it
Receive the bar with knees bent in a partial squat
Stand to lockout with bar stacked over heels
biomechanical_why: The push jerk combines the leg drive of the jerk with a symmetric receiving position, requiring simultaneous lower body power and upper body lock-out in the same plane of motion. The deltoid (anterior, lateral; origin: clavicle and acromion; insertion: deltoid tuberosity of humerus) and upper trapezius contribute to pressing the bar into the overhead position, while the triceps provide terminal elbow extension. The receiving squat position demands coordinated co-contraction of the quadriceps and hamstrings to create knee stability, and the core musculature maintains spinal alignment to transfer force from the legs to the bar overhead.

name: Squat Clean
technical_cues: Pull the bar fast and drop into a full squat simultaneously
Keep elbows driving up throughout the squat descent
Achieve depth with bar in the front rack before standing
Drive out of the hole with vertical torso
Stand fully before transitioning to the jerk
biomechanical_why: The squat clean demands the bar be caught in a full front squat position, recruiting the quadriceps, adductors, and hip flexors simultaneously to decelerate the descent while maintaining a high elbow position. The front rack position places significant demand on the thoracic extensors (erector spinae group) to maintain an upright torso, as any forward lean would cause the bar to roll off the deltoids and forward. Rising from the bottom of the front squat engages the gluteus maximus and vastus lateralis (origin: intertrochanteric line; insertion: lateral aspect of patella) eccentrically then concentrically, with the vastus medialis playing a critical role in knee stability during terminal extension.

name: Muscle Clean
technical_cues: Pull the bar from the floor without any rebend of the knees
Keep arms pulling throughout the entire movement
Drive elbows under the bar before it stops rising
Finish with bar in front rack and fully upright posture
Control the descent back to the hang position
biomechanical_why: The muscle clean removes the re-bending of the knees after the first pull, forcing the upper body pulling muscles—biceps brachii (origin: coracoid process and supraglenoid tubercle; insertion: radial tuberosity), brachialis, and brachioradialis—to contribute significantly more to bar elevation. The deltoids and rotator cuff must work to actively guide the bar path in an arc from thigh to shoulder, which develops positional awareness critical for the full power clean. This movement is commonly used as a technique drill because it exposes any timing deficiencies in the elbow flip and front rack receiving position without the complexity of the explosive rebend.

name: Clean Pull
technical_cues: Set up identically to the full clean with bar over mid-foot
Maintain bar contact with the legs through the first and second pull
Achieve maximum triple extension at the top
Shrug at the top and keep elbows high briefly
Lower the bar under control back to the floor
biomechanical_why: The clean pull trains the posterior chain through the full range of the clean's pull phase without the complexity of catching the bar, allowing heavier loads to be used for developing pulling strength. The erector spinae complex (origin: sacrum; insertion: thoracic and cervical vertebrae) maintains lumbar extension under load throughout the movement, and the quadratus lumborum (origin: iliac crest; insertion: 12th rib and lumbar transverse processes) provides lateral stability. The gastrocnemius and soleus (origin: posterior femoral condyles and posterior tibia/fibula; insertion: calcaneus via Achilles tendon) contribute ankle plantar flexion during triple extension, contributing meaningfully to bar acceleration.

name: Snatch Pull
technical_cues: Use a snatch-width grip and set up over the bar
Initiate with leg drive keeping the back angle constant
Sweep the bar in close contact with the thighs
Extend aggressively through ankles, knees, and hips
Finish with a powerful shoulder shrug and keep pulling
biomechanical_why: The snatch pull's wider grip alters the mechanics relative to the clean pull, requiring greater internal rotation of the shoulder throughout the first pull phase and greater lat engagement (latissimus dorsi; origin: spinous processes T6–T12, iliac crest; insertion: intertubercular groove of humerus) to keep the bar close to the body. The wider grip also changes the moment arm at the hip, placing slightly higher mechanical demand on the gluteus maximus and hamstrings during the second pull compared to the clean pull. As an overload accessory, the snatch pull allows athletes to handle supramaximal loads relative to their snatch maximum, building pulling strength and positional strength specific to the snatch bar path.

name: Hang Clean
technical_cues: Stand tall with the bar at arms' length, then hinge to mid-thigh
Engage the lats to prevent the bar from swinging away
Drive the hips to full extension before pulling under
Catch the bar in the front rack at any legal receiving depth
Keep the feet flat during the catch and stand to full extension
biomechanical_why: The hang clean develops explosive hip extension from a pre-loaded hamstring position, with the hamstring complex (biceps femoris long head; origin: ischial tuberosity; insertion: fibular head) storing elastic energy in the hinge before the aggressive concentric contraction. By eliminating the floor pull, the hang clean increases volume of training on the most critical portion of the lift—the second pull—which is where peak power is generated through rapid hip extension and shoulder shrug. The anterior deltoid and biceps brachii work together to guide the bar into the front rack receiving position by externally rotating the forearms and driving the elbows forward under the bar.

name: Hang Snatch
technical_cues: Take a wide snatch grip and hinge to upper thigh
Keep shoulder blades retracted and lats fired throughout
Explode hips to full extension then pull the body under
Punch both hands overhead before the bar reaches peak height
Receive with arms locked out and feet in a stable base
biomechanical_why: The hang snatch from above the knee emphasizes the explosive hip drive of the second pull and transitions quickly into an overhead receiving position, requiring exceptional rate of force development through the gluteus maximus and hamstrings. The wide grip creates a longer moment arm for internal shoulder rotators, meaning the subscapularis (origin: subscapular fossa; insertion: lesser tubercle of humerus) and pectoralis minor must work eccentrically to allow the bar to swing into the overhead lockout position safely. Overhead stability demands continuous co-contraction of all four rotator cuff muscles, the serratus anterior, and the lower trapezius to maintain the glenohumeral joint in its optimal position under dynamic load.

name: High Pull
technical_cues: Begin with clean or snatch grip depending on training goal
Drive through the legs aggressively to initiate bar speed
Shrug explosively then pull elbows high and wide
Allow the bar to reach sternum or chin height
Lower the bar under control with a slight knee bend
biomechanical_why: The high pull is an intermediate teaching progression that develops the shrug and upper pull mechanics without requiring the athlete to receive the bar, making it valuable for building pulling strength and technique concurrently. The upper trapezius (origin: external occipital protuberance; insertion: lateral clavicle) and levator scapulae (origin: transverse processes C1–C4; insertion: superior angle of scapula) work intensively during the shrug phase to elevate the scapula and transmit force from the lower body into the bar. The deltoids and biceps brachii contribute to the arm pull component, with the brachioradialis (origin: lateral supracondylar ridge of humerus; insertion: radial styloid process) providing elbow flexion speed to achieve maximum bar height.

name: Snatch Deadlift
technical_cues: Set up with a wide snatch grip, bar over mid-foot
Push the floor away rather than pulling the bar up
Maintain the same back angle through the first pull phase
Keep the bar dragging along the legs throughout the lift
Lock out hips and knees simultaneously at the top
biomechanical_why: The snatch deadlift uses the wide snatch grip which drops the hips lower relative to the conventional deadlift, activating the quadriceps more forcefully through the initial phase while still heavily recruiting the posterior chain. The wide grip increases the moment arm at the shoulder, requiring greater engagement of the latissimus dorsi and teres major (origin: inferior angle of scapula; insertion: intertubercular groove of humerus) to maintain the bar close to the body and prevent it from drifting forward. The snatch deadlift serves as a supplementary strength exercise to overload the pulling pattern of the snatch, building positional strength at the floor and through the difficult transition from first to second pull.

name: Log Press
technical_cues: Clean the log to your chest with neutral grip handles
Set elbows high and tuck them slightly under the log
Dip and drive with a vertical torso before pressing
Press aggressively while locking out elbows at full extension
Control the descent back to the chest between reps
biomechanical_why: The log press differs from a barbell press because the neutral grip (palms facing each other) places the shoulder in a mechanically advantaged position by reducing impingement risk at the subacromial space, allowing the supraspinatus (origin: supraspinous fossa; insertion: greater tubercle) to function more efficiently. The wider and thicker implement shifts the center of mass forward, requiring greater anterior deltoid recruitment and core bracing through the transverse abdominis (origin: inner iliac crest and thoracolumbar fascia; insertion: linea alba) to prevent lumbar hyperextension. The initial leg drive in the push-press style log press engages the quadriceps and gastrocnemius to create vertical momentum that reduces the muscular demand on the pressing muscles at the most mechanically disadvantaged portion of the press.

name: Atlas Stone Lift
technical_cues: Approach the stone in a wide sumo stance, feet beside it
Lap the stone to your thighs before driving to the chest
Use a strong hip extension to elevate the stone to chest height
Drive through the hips and lean back slightly to load the platform
Release the stone at the top of the extension
biomechanical_why: The atlas stone lift is a full-body extension pattern that uniquely taxes the thoracic extensors and deep lumbar stabilizers due to the rounded implement requiring a flexed-spine lifting position unlike any barbell movement. The biceps brachii and forearm flexors must maintain a crushing grip around a large sphere, making forearm endurance a significant limiting factor as the wrist flexors (flexor digitorum superficialis; origin: medial epicondyle; insertion: middle phalanges) work near maximal capacity. Hip extension power from the gluteus maximus drives the stone upward once lapped, and the rectus abdominis and obliques must rapidly transition from flexion support to extension bracing to protect the lumbar spine during the final load-over phase.

name: Yoke Walk
technical_cues: Set the yoke height so pads rest across the upper traps
Take short, quick steps to maintain momentum under load
Keep the core braced maximally throughout the carry
Look straight ahead and avoid lateral trunk shifting
Drive the knees forward aggressively with each step
biomechanical_why: The yoke walk places an axial compressive load across the upper trapezius and cervical spine, requiring extraordinary isometric strength from the trapezius, rhomboids (origin: spinous processes C7–T5; insertion: medial border of scapula), and deep cervical stabilizers to prevent lateral neck flexion under extreme loads. The compressive spinal loading during walking demands the lumbar multifidus (origin: sacrum and articular processes; insertion: spinous processes 2–4 levels above) to fire maximally to maintain intervertebral stability with each asymmetric footfall. Locomotion under supramaximal spinal loading creates unique co-contraction demands across the hip abductors, adductors, and core, developing functional strength transfer to sport and daily movement patterns.

name: Tire Flip
technical_cues: Position hands under the tire at low level, not on the tread
Drive the tire with legs first, keeping back straight
Transition hands to the tread as the tire rises past your knees
Lean into the tire and push with full body extension
Follow through until the tire falls forward completely
biomechanical_why: The tire flip mimics a deadlift-to-hip-extension-to-push pattern, engaging the posterior chain through the initial lift phase before transitioning to an explosive push requiring pectorals (pectoralis major; origin: clavicle, sternum, and costal cartilages; insertion: greater tubercle of humerus) and anterior deltoids. The unstable, heavy rubber implement creates variable resistance through the movement arc, meaning the gluteus maximus and hamstrings must generate force through an extended range of hip extension before the quadriceps and hip flexors assist in the forward lean and push-over. Grip strength (finger and wrist flexors) is uniquely taxed because the tire's wide rubber surface prevents a conventional grip, forcing the athlete to use friction-based palmar contact instead.

name: Farmers Walk (Heavy)
technical_cues: Grip the handles with a crush grip before picking up
Deadlift the implements straight up with hips and back
Walk with quick, deliberate steps and tall posture
Keep shoulders packed down and back throughout the carry
Brake the implements under control at the end of the run
biomechanical_why: The heavy farmers walk creates bilateral axial loading that taxes the spinal erectors, quadratus lumborum, and deep multifidus equally under a walking gait pattern, challenging spinal stability in a way that static exercises cannot replicate. The trapezius (middle and lower) must actively depress the scapulae to prevent the heavy load from pulling the shoulders into elevation and forward translation, while the rhomboids maintain scapular retraction against gravity. Grip strength endurance is the primary performance limiter in competition settings, with the flexor digitorum profundus (origin: anterior and medial ulna; insertion: distal phalanges) and flexor digitorum superficialis sustaining near-maximal isometric contractions for the duration of the carry.

name: Axle Bar Deadlift
technical_cues: Use a double overhand or mixed grip on the thick 2-inch bar
Expect the bar to float away from the hands—squeeze harder
Set up and pull identically to a conventional deadlift
Brace the core before breaking the bar from the floor
Lock hips and knees out simultaneously at the top
biomechanical_why: The axle bar's 2-inch diameter compared to a standard 1.1-inch barbell dramatically increases the grip moment arm, forcing the finger flexors and intrinsic hand muscles (lumbricals; origin: flexor digitorum profundus tendons; insertion: extensor expansion of fingers) to generate significantly more force just to prevent the bar from rolling out of the hand. This grip demand pre-fatigues the forearm flexors before the primary movers (gluteus maximus, hamstrings, erector spinae) are stressed, making the axle deadlift an excellent grip and posterior chain developer simultaneously. The lack of knurling in some axle implementations further increases the friction demand, training the deep finger flexors in a way that transfers directly to stone, log, and circus dumbbell events.

name: Circus Dumbbell Press
technical_cues: Clean the dumbbell to the shoulder in one explosive motion
Keep the dumbbell head balanced before initiating the press
Use a push-press drive from the legs to initiate the press
Press to full lockout with the wrist stacked under the dumbbell
Lower the dumbbell under control back to the shoulder
biomechanical_why: The circus dumbbell's offset weight distribution (with weight loaded on one side of a thicker handle) creates a constantly rotating torque about the wrist, requiring intense co-contraction of the wrist flexors and extensors (extensor carpi radialis longus; origin: lateral supracondylar ridge; insertion: base of 2nd metacarpal) to maintain a neutral wrist position throughout the press. The single-arm nature of the lift creates a significant lateral trunk stability demand, requiring maximal activation of the ipsilateral quadratus lumborum and contralateral obliques to prevent lateral trunk flexion under load. The anterior deltoid and upper pectoralis major are primary movers through the press arc, while the supraspinatus initiates the first 15–30 degrees of abduction required to clear the shoulder joint.

name: Keg Carry
technical_cues: Hug the keg to your chest with forearms beneath it
Lift the keg by driving through the hips, not the lower back
Keep the keg pressed tightly to your torso while walking
Use short strides to maintain balance with the shifting load
Set the keg down by squatting rather than bending forward
biomechanical_why: The keg carry's shifting liquid contents create dynamic instability, requiring continuous micro-adjustments from the core musculature—specifically the transverse abdominis and internal obliques—to prevent the liquid momentum from pulling the carrier off balance. The bilateral bear-hug grip with forearms underneath activates the biceps brachii and brachialis isometrically, while the anterior deltoids must maintain a forward reach position against the keg's gravitational pull downward and away. The forearm supinators (supinator; origin: lateral epicondyle and proximal ulna; insertion: proximal radius) work intensively to maintain the forearm-under position against the downward load of the keg pressing on the forearms.

name: Sandbag Clean
technical_cues: Set up in a wide stance with the bag positioned between the feet
Grip the top handles or hug the bag firmly before pulling
Drive through the legs and hips to accelerate the bag upward
Guide the bag to a shoulder or chest rack position
Control the bag to prevent it shifting mid-movement
biomechanical_why: The sandbag's shifting contents reduce the predictability of the load's center of mass during the clean, demanding greater stabilizer activation throughout the movement compared to a barbell clean of equivalent weight. The trapezius and rhomboids must work harder to maintain scapular position because the bag's soft surface prevents a consistent hand position, forcing the upper back to compensate for grip angle variations. Racking the sandbag to the shoulder loads the anterior deltoid, supraspinatus, and biceps brachii isometrically in an end-range position while the core must prevent lateral flexion to keep the bag stable against the trunk.

name: Sandbag Carry
technical_cues: Pick up the sandbag and position it on one or both shoulders
Engage the core firmly before taking the first step
Maintain upright posture and avoid excessive forward lean
Keep the bag pressed against the body to reduce leverage
Move at a controlled pace to keep the bag from shifting
biomechanical_why: Carrying a sandbag on the shoulder creates a significant asymmetric loading pattern that challenges the contralateral quadratus lumborum (origin: posterior iliac crest; insertion: 12th rib and transverse processes of L1–L4) and ipsilateral hip abductors to maintain pelvis levelness throughout the gait cycle. The continuously shifting contents of the bag require the deep spinal stabilizers—multifidus and rotatores (origin: transverse processes; insertion: spinous process of vertebra above)—to perform rapid reactive co-contractions to prevent spinal rotation with every footfall. Shoulder stability is provided by an isometric contraction of the middle and lower trapezius to prevent the scapula from winging under the lateral compressive load.

name: Loading Race
technical_cues: Approach each implement from the hip, not by bending the spine
Generate explosive hip extension for every lift and carry
Sprint between implements with a strong arm drive
Load implements over the bar with hip extension, not arm elevation
Maintain consistent breathing and bracing throughout
biomechanical_why: The loading race is a repeated maximal effort event where the athlete must perform multiple explosive deadlift-and-carry cycles with varied implements, creating cumulative fatigue across the posterior chain—specifically the gluteus maximus, erector spinae, and hamstrings—that tests muscular endurance rather than single-effort strength. The hip flexors (iliopsoas; origin: T12-L5 and iliac fossa; insertion: lesser trochanter of femur) experience significant eccentric loading during the sprint transitions, as the athlete must rapidly decelerate and change direction before each implement pickup. Cardiovascular and metabolic demands place loading races in a unique training category where anaerobic glycolysis must sustain repeated near-maximal efforts with minimal rest, differentiating the event from pure strength work.

name: Arm-Over-Arm Rope Pull
technical_cues: Sit low on the ground with feet braced against a solid object
Pull the rope with alternating hands in a rhythmic pattern
Drive each pull with the full lat and bicep, not just the forearm
Keep the body stable and avoid rocking the torso excessively
Maintain a strong grip throughout the entire duration
biomechanical_why: The arm-over-arm rope pull is a bilateral alternating pulling exercise that primarily recruits the latissimus dorsi, teres major, and biceps brachii through a shoulder extension and elbow flexion pattern, generating force to move a heavy sled or vehicle. The seated position eliminates lower body contribution, placing the full demand on the upper body pulling musculature and creating a direct training stimulus for pulling strength development without requiring leg drive. Grip endurance is a critical performance factor, with the flexor digitorum profundus performing repeated near-maximal contractions through hundreds of individual pulls, making forearm muscular endurance highly specific to this event.

name: Viking Press
technical_cues: Set grip on the handles with a neutral or pronated position
Drive the implement overhead with a full leg push-press drive
Lock out both elbows at the top of each repetition
Lower the handles under control to the start position
Keep the core braced between every repetition
biomechanical_why: The Viking press uses a fixed-implement pressing machine that allows heavier loads than a strict press because the athlete can use leg drive to initiate each repetition, engaging the quadriceps and gluteus maximus before the deltoids and triceps take over through the pressing range. The fixed handle position on most Viking press implements keeps the wrists in a neutral or slightly pronated position, reducing radial deviation stress and allowing a more powerful grip compared to a straight barbell. Because reps are performed rapidly in competition, the stretch-shortening cycle of the deltoids and triceps (origin: infraglenoid tubercle; insertion: olecranon) is exploited by the brief reversal at the bottom, contributing to higher repetition totals than pure concentric pressing.

name: Stone to Shoulder
technical_cues: Lap the stone first by rolling it onto your thighs
Drive explosively through the hips to elevate the stone to chest
Quickly shift hands underneath and behind the stone
Lean back and drive the stone up and over to one shoulder
Squeeze the stone against your body throughout the movement
biomechanical_why: The stone-to-shoulder movement loads the biceps brachii and forearm flexors in an extreme supinated grip as the athlete wraps their arms around the stone, creating high tension in the musculotendinous junction and bicipital tendon during the initial lapping phase. The transition from the chest to the shoulder requires rapid shoulder external rotation and abduction, primarily through the posterior deltoid (origin: scapular spine; insertion: deltoid tuberosity) and supraspinatus, while maintaining the crushing grip around the stone's surface. The lumbar extensors perform heavy eccentric work during the leaning-back phase before the stone reaches the shoulder, with the erector spinae operating at long muscle lengths under compressive spinal loads.

name: Car Deadlift Simulation
technical_cues: Grip the fixed handles at approximately mid-thigh height
Set the back in a strong hinge position before the pull
Drive through the floor with both legs simultaneously
Extend hips and knees to achieve full lockout
Control the lowering phase back to the starting position
biomechanical_why: The car deadlift simulation uses a fixed-frame implement that removes the need to manage bar path, allowing the athlete to focus on generating maximum vertical force through the legs and hips against an extremely heavy load at a specific handle height. The fixed handle height often places the starting position higher than a conventional deadlift, shifting more load to the hip extensors (gluteus maximus, semimembranosus) and reducing quadriceps involvement compared to a floor deadlift. The implement's rigidity and fixed handles allow the athlete to grip maximally without worrying about barbell rotation or flex, directing all muscular effort into the primary movers of hip and knee extension through the posterior and anterior chain.

name: Duck Walk
technical_cues: Place the implement on the upper back or shoulders before walking
Descend into a deep squat before taking the first step
Walk forward in a full squat position, maintaining depth
Keep the torso as upright as possible throughout
Breathe continuously and do not hold the breath for entire sets
biomechanical_why: The duck walk performed in a deep squat position places the knee at or near maximum flexion throughout the gait cycle, creating extreme eccentric and isometric demand on the quadriceps—particularly the rectus femoris (origin: anterior inferior iliac spine; insertion: tibial tuberosity via patellar tendon) which spans two joints and must elongate while producing force. The deep hip flexion position reduces the mechanical advantage of the gluteus maximus significantly, shifting more of the locomotor burden to the quadriceps and hip adductors (adductor magnus; origin: ischiopubic ramus and ischial tuberosity; insertion: linea aspera and adductor tubercle). The upright posture requirement forces the deep hip flexors and core to work intensively to prevent the torso from collapsing forward, making the movement a comprehensive lower body and trunk endurance challenge.

name: Hummer Tire Deadlift
technical_cues: Stand between or behind the tire with hands on a fixed handle bar
Establish a strong hip hinge position before initiating the pull
Drive the floor away and extend hips aggressively to lockout
Maintain lumbar extension throughout the pull
Lower the implement under control for repeated efforts
biomechanical_why: The Hummer tire deadlift typically uses a frame or harness attached to an extremely heavy tire, creating a deadlift pattern with loads often exceeding world-record conventional deadlift weights due to the fixed handle and momentum assist. The posterior chain engagement is similar to a conventional deadlift—gluteus maximus, biceps femoris, and erector spinae being primary movers—but the absolute loads recruited create extraordinary mechanical tension in the myosin-actin cross-bridges that conventional training cannot achieve. The fixed handle position removes the requirement for grip strength as a limiting factor, allowing greater expression of hip and back strength and making this event a true test of posterior chain maximal force production.

name: Block Press
technical_cues: Clean or load the block to the chest before pressing
Set the grip firmly around the implement's handles or frame
Dip and drive with the legs to initiate the press
Press aggressively to full lockout overhead
Control the block back to the chest without bouncing
biomechanical_why: The block press places a large rectangular implement in front of the body, shifting the load anteriorly compared to a log press, which increases the demand on the anterior deltoids and upper pectoralis major (origin: medial clavicle; insertion: greater tubercle of humerus) to prevent the implement from pulling the torso forward. The wide grip required by most block implements places the shoulder abductors at a mechanical advantage, reducing subacromial compression and allowing greater force production through the pressing arc. The leg drive component of the push-press style block press recruits the soleus and gastrocnemius to contribute ankle plantar flexion at the initiation, contributing to the upward momentum before the upper body pressing muscles take over.

name: Keg Toss
technical_cues: Grip the keg firmly around the body with both hands
Generate power from a hip hinge and explosive hip extension
Drive the keg upward and backward in an arcing trajectory
Release the keg at the optimal angle for maximum height
Follow through and maintain balance after the release
biomechanical_why: The keg toss is a ballistic posterior chain power exercise where the gluteus maximus and hamstrings perform rapid concentric contraction from a hip hinge position to generate the upward throwing momentum. The explosive triple extension pattern is identical mechanically to the second pull of an Olympic lift, making the keg toss an excellent power output measure and training tool for hip extension rate of force development. The release requires coordinated relaxation of the hip and trunk extensors while the anterior deltoids guide the trajectory, and the wrist flexors and extensors must fluidly release the implement at the peak of its momentum to maximize distance or height.

name: Super Yoke
technical_cues: Set the yoke slightly higher than a standard yoke to accommodate weight
Brace the entire core maximally before lifting off
Take the shortest, quickest steps possible under the load
Keep eyes forward and avoid lateral head movement
Breathe in short bursts between steps rather than holding breath
biomechanical_why: The super yoke uses loads substantially above standard yoke weights, creating extreme axial compressive forces on the intervertebral discs that require maximal intra-abdominal pressure (generated by the transverse abdominis and diaphragm) as a pneumatic protective mechanism for the spine. The upper trapezius and levator scapulae work isometrically to prevent the yoke pads from crushing the cervical spine into lateral flexion, while the cervical multifidus provides segmental stability at each cervical vertebral level. The foot strike pattern under supramaximal loads creates rapid ground reaction forces that must be absorbed and transmitted through the hip extensors and knee stabilizers before being redirected into the next propulsive step.

name: Trap Bar Deadlift
technical_cues: Step inside the hex bar and position feet hip-width apart
Hinge to grip the handles with a neutral grip
Drive the floor away with both legs while keeping the back flat
Extend hips and knees simultaneously to achieve lockout
Lower by hinging the hips back first, then bending the knees
biomechanical_why: The trap bar deadlift places the load directly at the athlete's center of gravity rather than in front of it, reducing the moment arm at the hip and lumbar spine compared to the conventional deadlift and allowing greater quadriceps contribution from the vastus group through the movement. The neutral grip handles reduce forearm pronation stress and allow the biceps brachii to contribute to grip stability more effectively than a prone grip barbell, making the trap bar deadlift more accessible for athletes with shoulder or forearm impairments. Studies demonstrate that the trap bar deadlift produces greater peak force and velocity than conventional deadlift at the same absolute load, making it a highly effective tool for developing lower body power and strength.

name: Sumo Deadlift (Heavy)
technical_cues: Set a wide stance with toes pointed out 30–45 degrees
Grip the bar narrow, inside the legs with arms straight
Break the floor by pushing feet outward, not pressing down
Keep the bar dragging up the shins and over the knees
Lock hips through aggressively at the top of the lift
biomechanical_why: The sumo deadlift uses a wide stance that internally rotates the femur and externally rotates the tibia, placing the hip adductors (adductor magnus, adductor longus; origin: pubic ramus and ischial tuberosity; insertion: linea aspera and adductor tubercle) in a pre-stretched position that contributes significantly to the initial breakaway force from the floor. The upright torso of the sumo stance reduces the moment arm at the lumbar spine compared to conventional, decreasing erector spinae demand but increasing the relative hip abductor requirement from the gluteus medius and minimus to maintain femoral alignment throughout the pull. The narrow grip inside the legs allows the bar to travel a shorter distance to lockout compared to a conventional deadlift at the same starting position, which is why sumo-style lifters can often move heavier weights.

name: Conventional Deadlift
technical_cues: Set feet hip-width with bar over the mid-foot
Grip the bar just outside the legs with a hook or mixed grip
Drop the hips to create tension before pulling
Drag the bar up the shins keeping the lats engaged
Drive hips through to full extension at lockout
biomechanical_why: The conventional deadlift is the most biomechanically demanding posterior chain exercise, placing significant mechanical load on the erector spinae (origin: sacrum and posterior ilium; insertion: ribs, transverse processes, and occipital bone) throughout the entire range of motion due to the horizontal moment arm created by the barbell's position in front of the body. The gluteus maximus is the primary hip extensor, and the hamstrings function as secondary hip extensors and play a crucial role in controlling the forward trunk lean during the first pull by eccentrically decelerating hip flexion momentum. The latissimus dorsi must contract isometrically throughout the lift to maintain the bar against the shins and prevent it from drifting forward, which would exponentially increase the lumbar moment arm and risk of injury.

name: Romanian Deadlift (Heavy)
technical_cues: Begin standing with the bar at full hip extension
Hinge the hips back while keeping a micro-bend in the knees
Lower the bar along the legs until a hamstring stretch is felt
Drive the hips forward forcefully to return to standing
Maintain lumbar extension throughout the entire movement
biomechanical_why: The Romanian deadlift (RDL) is a hip hinge pattern that places the hamstrings under maximum eccentric and isometric load through an extended range, as the knee remains nearly fully extended throughout the movement, preventing the biceps femoris short head from contributing to knee flexion assistance. The gluteus maximus is the primary driver of the concentric hip extension phase, with the hamstring complex providing critical tension to maintain pelvic positioning during the descent phase and acting as secondary extensors during the ascent. The erector spinae work isometrically throughout the movement to maintain lumbar lordosis against the flexion moment created by the heavy load, making heavy RDLs an excellent developer of spinal erector endurance under load.

name: Paused Squat
technical_cues: Descend under control to the prescribed depth
Pause for a full 2–3 seconds at the bottom without relaxing
Maintain brace and upright torso throughout the pause
Drive out of the hole immediately after the pause ends
Do not bounce or use the stretch reflex to initiate the ascent
biomechanical_why: The paused squat eliminates the stretch-shortening cycle by requiring a static hold at the bottom position, meaning the ascending force must be generated entirely by concentric muscle action without the elastic energy stored by the myosin cross-bridges during an active descent. The pause places extreme isometric demand on the quadriceps—particularly the vastus medialis oblique (VMO), which is most active at deep knee flexion angles—and the gluteus maximus at long muscle lengths where the force-velocity relationship is least favorable. Maintaining lumbar extension during the pause requires sustained maximal activation of the deep spinal stabilizers (multifidus and erector spinae), building positional strength and body awareness that transfers to improved squat mechanics under heavy loads.

name: Box Squat
technical_cues: Set the box height to parallel or slightly below
Sit back onto the box until the full hips contact the surface
Release all hip flexor tension briefly before the concentric
Drive the knees out aggressively as you stand
Keep the shin vertical and avoid knee drift forward
biomechanical_why: The box squat forces a more horizontal shin angle than a free squat, which reduces the moment arm at the knee but significantly increases the moment arm at the hip, shifting the primary loading from the quadriceps to the gluteus maximus, adductors, and posterior chain. The brief sit on the box breaks the eccentric-to-concentric coupling, requiring the athlete to initiate the ascent from a dead stop without stretch reflex assistance, which builds superior concentric starting strength in the hip extensors. The hip flexors (iliopsoas and rectus femoris) are placed at maximum stretch during the sit phase, and the subsequent explosive hip extension activates the stretch reflex of these muscles to create additional force production during the ascent.

name: Safety Bar Squat
technical_cues: Place the safety bar yoke across the upper traps and hold the handles
Keep elbows down and forward to maintain the bar position
Descend with a controlled tempo, sitting the hips back slightly
Drive the knees out as you descend and ascend
Push through the heels and maintain an upright torso
biomechanical_why: The safety squat bar's forward-angled yoke creates a moment arm that pulls the torso forward, requiring the upper back extensors—primarily the thoracic erector spinae and middle trapezius—to work significantly harder than in a high-bar squat to maintain an upright position. This forward-pull tendency means the rectus abdominis and external obliques must also co-contract to prevent lumbar flexion, making the safety bar squat a superior upper back and core builder compared to standard barbell squats. The handles allow athletes with limited shoulder mobility or upper body injuries to squat safely with axial loading, expanding the population that can train the squat pattern productively.

name: Zercher Squat
technical_cues: Load the bar in the elbow crease with elbows tight together
Hold the bar close to the body throughout the entire squat
Descend with an upright torso due to the anterior load position
Keep the elbows pressed together and up throughout
Drive the hips forward and elbows up to stand
biomechanical_why: The Zercher squat's elbow position creates an anterior loading pattern similar to a front squat but held at a lower position, which reduces the lever arm on the upper spine while simultaneously requiring the biceps brachii and brachialis to work intensively isometrically to prevent the bar from rolling down the forearms. The anterior load placement in the elbow crease forces a more upright torso than a back squat, increasing quadriceps demand (particularly vastus lateralis and rectus femoris) through the full range of knee flexion while reducing hip extensor moment arm. The elbow compression against the barbell creates a unique challenge for the forearm flexors (flexor carpi radialis and ulnaris; origin: medial epicondyle; insertion: metacarpal bases) which must maintain a pain-resistant grip position under significant compressive load.

name: Anderson Squat
technical_cues: Set the barbell on pins at or just below parallel depth
Position the body under the bar in the bottom squat position
Brace maximally before beginning the ascent
Drive straight up from the dead stop position
Reset the bar on the pins between each repetition
biomechanical_why: The Anderson squat eliminates the eccentric phase entirely, requiring the athlete to initiate from a dead stop at or near the most mechanically disadvantaged position of the squat, which develops concentric starting strength in the quadriceps and gluteus maximus without stretch-shortening cycle assistance. The dead-stop initiation necessitates maximum motor unit recruitment at the beginning of each repetition, which has superior neurological training benefits for developing rate of force development compared to the continuous tension of standard squats. Setting up from below a bar on pins also improves proprioceptive awareness of torso and foot position at depth, as the athlete must learn to align optimally before each attempt to produce sufficient force from the compromised position.

name: Belt Squat
technical_cues: Attach the loading chain to the belt at the center of the hips
Step onto the elevated platform with feet hip-width apart
Descend freely with load hanging from the hips, not the spine
Keep torso upright and hands free or held on handles
Drive through both feet to stand to full hip extension
biomechanical_why: The belt squat removes all axial spinal loading by anchoring the weight to the hips via a belt, allowing the quadriceps, gluteus maximus, and hamstrings to be trained to near-maximum effort without compressive or shear forces on the lumbar spine, making it ideal for athletes managing back injuries. The hanging load causes the pelvis to posteriorly tilt slightly compared to a barbell squat, which alters the moment arm of the hip extensors and can provide a unique training stimulus for the deep gluteal musculature (gluteus medius and minimus; origin: gluteal surface of ilium; insertion: greater trochanter). The free upper body position of the belt squat allows athletes to focus entirely on lower body mechanics without compensating for thoracic or shoulder mobility limitations that often affect barbell squat form.

name: Deficit Deadlift
technical_cues: Stand on a 1–4 inch plate or platform to increase the range
Set up as normal with bar over mid-foot and strong hip hinge
Maintain a lower hip position throughout the first pull
Drive the floor away with maximum leg force
Lockout is identical to a conventional deadlift
biomechanical_why: The deficit deadlift increases the range of motion compared to conventional by starting with the bar lower relative to the hips, which requires the lifter to be in a deeper hip and knee flexion position at the start, increasing quadriceps activation through the critical initial pull phase. The extended range of motion places the hamstrings at an even greater stretch at the beginning of the lift, increasing elastic energy storage and potentially augmenting the force available during the initial breakaway from the floor. The greater bottom-end challenge of the deficit deadlift makes it an excellent supplementary exercise for strengthening the weakest portion of the conventional or sumo deadlift for athletes who struggle to break the bar from the floor.

name: Block Pull
technical_cues: Set blocks to the desired height, typically knee height or below
Approach and grip identically to the competition deadlift stance
Initiate the pull as if starting a deadlift from that height
Focus on accelerating the bar through the sticking point
Hold the lockout for a full second before lowering
biomechanical_why: The block pull reduces the range of motion by starting the bar above the floor, which allows supramaximal loading (above competition deadlift maximum) to be used and specifically trains the upper half or middle section of the deadlift pull where the moment arm is still significant. The shorter range of motion means the quadriceps contribute relatively less to block pulls than floor deadlifts, shifting more demand onto the gluteus maximus and erector spinae through the hip drive phase from knee height to lockout. Block pulls at knee height target the transition zone where many lifters experience a sticking point, building lockout strength and teaching the motor pattern of hip drive and knee track-back that is essential for completing heavy conventional deadlifts.

name: Pause Deadlift
technical_cues: Set up and initiate the pull identically to competition deadlift
Pause the bar at just below the knee for 2–3 seconds
Maintain full tension and body position during the pause
Continue driving through the floor after the pause ends
Do not relax any segment of the body during the hold
biomechanical_why: The pause at the knee position of the deadlift targets the most mechanically difficult transition in the lift, where the bar must pass around the knee joint and the moment arm at the hip is near maximum, requiring sustained isometric force from the erector spinae, gluteus maximus, and hamstrings simultaneously. The isometric hold at the knee eliminates momentum assistance that would normally carry the bar through this point, forcing greater motor unit recruitment in the deep spinal stabilizers and hip extensors to maintain position against gravity. This pause forces athletes to develop awareness of the scapular depression cue (via lower trapezius and latissimus dorsi) needed to keep the bar close to the body at the transition, which is where forward drift most commonly occurs in fatigued lifters.

name: Stiff-Leg Deadlift (Heavy)
technical_cues: Keep legs nearly locked out throughout the entire movement
Hinge the hips back to lower the bar along the legs
Maintain lumbar extension at all points of the movement
Drive the hips forward powerfully on the ascent
Stop the range of motion when hamstring flexibility is the limit
biomechanical_why: The stiff-leg deadlift with locked knees places the hamstrings (biceps femoris, semimembranosus, semitendinosus) under maximal eccentric tension through the greatest possible range, as the lack of knee flexion prevents the hamstrings from shortening from the distal end and forces the entire length change to occur at the hip. The straight-leg position also creates the longest possible moment arm at the hip joint for the load, maximizing the eccentric force on the hamstring musculotendinous unit, which is a primary driver of hypertrophy and strength adaptations in this muscle group. The erector spinae must work even harder than in a Romanian deadlift to prevent lumbar flexion at the bottom position because of the greater forward lean required when the knees are fully extended.

name: Barbell Hip Thrust (Heavy)
technical_cues: Place upper back on a bench with bar across the hip crease
Use padding to protect the hip bones under the bar
Drive through both heels to thrust the hips to the ceiling
Achieve full hip extension with a neutral spine at the top
Lower under control until the plates touch the floor
biomechanical_why: The barbell hip thrust places the gluteus maximus in its most mechanically advantaged position for producing force—at full hip extension—where the muscle operates at an optimal length-tension relationship and the moment arm of the load is maximized relative to the hip joint center. Research demonstrates that the hip thrust produces greater peak gluteus maximus EMG amplitude than the barbell squat or deadlift, making it the most effective single exercise for targeting the gluteal musculature directly. The horizontal loading vector of the hip thrust (compared to the vertical vector of squats and deadlifts) creates a unique training stimulus that develops hip extension strength with minimal knee extensor or spinal erector co-loading, making it highly specific for sprint and jump power transfer.

name: Glute-Ham Raise (Weighted)
technical_cues: Position feet locked under the GHR pad with thighs on the bench
Lower the torso under full control to a horizontal position
Curl the body upward using the hamstrings and glutes
Hold a weight plate or dumbbell at the chest for added load
Avoid using hip flexors or momentum to complete each rep
biomechanical_why: The glute-ham raise is unique in that it trains the hamstrings through simultaneous hip extension and knee flexion actions, which requires the biceps femoris and semimembranosus to work across both their proximal (ischial tuberosity) and distal (fibular head and tibia) attachments under high tension, a bi-articular loading pattern unavailable in most other exercises. The eccentricphase of the weighted GHR taxes the hamstrings at long lengths under high tension, creating the mechanical environment most associated with protective adaptations against hamstring strain injury. The gluteus maximus contributes significantly to the hip extension component of the ascent, particularly in the final range of hip extension, while the erector spinae isometrically maintain the spinal position throughout the entire movement.

name: Barbell Good Morning (Heavy)
technical_cues: Place the barbell across the upper traps in a high-bar position
Hinge the hips back while maintaining lumbar extension
Lower the torso until it is near parallel to the floor
Drive the hips forward powerfully to return to standing
Keep a soft knee bend throughout to protect the hamstrings
biomechanical_why: The barbell good morning creates the longest possible moment arm for the load at the hip when the torso is near horizontal, placing the erector spinae (particularly the longissimus and iliocostalis) under extraordinary isometric and eccentric loading in their most vulnerable lengthened position. The hamstrings (semimembranosus and semitendinosus; origin: ischial tuberosity; insertion: proximal tibia) function as primary hip extensors during the concentric ascent, and the forward lean position places them at a significant stretch that increases the recruitment signal and may contribute to greater hypertrophic adaptations than conventional deadlift variations. Heavy good mornings are considered one of the most effective exercises for developing lumbar spine resilience when progressed appropriately, as the erector spinae must sustain high force production through their full range of motion.

name: Pendlay Row (Heavy)
technical_cues: Set up with a flat back parallel to the floor before each rep
Pull the bar from a dead stop on the floor each repetition
Drive elbows back and up in a straight line toward the hip
Touch the bar to the lower sternum or upper abdomen
Lower the bar completely to the floor between each rep
biomechanical_why: The Pendlay row's dead-stop initiation from the floor on each repetition eliminates the momentum generated by the bounce of a standard barbell row, requiring the latissimus dorsi (origin: spinous processes T6–T12; insertion: intertubercular groove of humerus) and posterior deltoid to generate maximal force from rest on every repetition, developing starting strength in the horizontal pull pattern. The strict horizontal torso position ensures that the rhomboids (origin: spinous processes C7–T5; insertion: medial border of scapula) and middle trapezius perform the majority of the scapular retraction work rather than being assisted by a slight torso swing, creating superior upper back hypertrophy stimulus. The biceps brachii (origin: coracoid process and supraglenoid tubercle; insertion: radial tuberosity) contributes significantly to elbow flexion throughout the pull, and the brachioradialis acts as a secondary elbow flexor that limits fatigue of the biceps in high-volume sets.

name: Barbell Shrug (Heavy)
technical_cues: Grip the bar with a shoulder-width double overhand grip
Stand tall with arms straight and bar hanging at arms' length
Elevate the shoulders straight up as high as possible
Hold briefly at the peak before lowering under control
Avoid rolling the shoulders in a circular motion
biomechanical_why: The barbell shrug directly targets the upper trapezius (origin: external occipital protuberance and nuchal ligament; insertion: posterior clavicle and acromion) through pure scapular elevation against a heavy load, which is the specific function of this muscle belly and difficult to achieve through compound movements alone. The levator scapulae (origin: transverse processes C1–C4; insertion: superior angle of scapula) co-contracts with the upper trapezius during the shrug, and both muscles contribute to neck protection under the heavy overhead compressive loads encountered in yoke walks and log presses. Heavy shrugs with supramaximal grip loads (using straps if necessary) also provide a significant isometric stimulus for the finger flexors and intrinsic hand muscles, contributing to the grip strength development important in all strongman events.

name: Power Shrug
technical_cues: Set up identically to a conventional deadlift
Pull the bar from the floor with explosive leg drive
Allow the legs to extend fully before the shrug begins
Shrug violently with a slight rise on the toes at peak
Lower the bar quickly back to the floor for the next rep
biomechanical_why: The power shrug combines the explosive posterior chain drive of a deadlift with the terminal upper trapezius shrug, mimicking the complete mechanical sequence of the power clean's second pull without the catch phase. The triple extension of ankle, knee, and hip during the pull phase recruits the gastrocnemius, quadriceps, and gluteus maximus in a powerful coordinated sequence before the upper trapezius fires to complete the movement, creating a training stimulus for the full Olympic pulling chain. The momentum generated by the lower body drive allows significantly heavier loads to be used in the shrug phase compared to a static barbell shrug, providing greater mechanical tension to the upper trapezius and levator scapulae and developing the rate of force development in the shrug that is critical for Olympic lifting success.

name: Snatch-Grip Deadlift
technical_cues: Take a wide snatch-width grip before approaching the bar
The hip position will be lower than a conventional deadlift
Maintain the wide grip and keep the bar close to the body
Drive through the legs with greater quadriceps contribution
Lock out hips fully while maintaining lat engagement throughout
biomechanical_why: The snatch-grip deadlift's wide hand placement dramatically increases the moment arm at the shoulder relative to the conventional deadlift, requiring the latissimus dorsi and teres major to generate significantly greater isometric force to keep the bar against the body and prevent forward drift throughout the pull. The wider grip also forces the hips into a lower starting position because the bar must travel a greater distance to reach lockout, which increases quadriceps activation during the initial drive off the floor compared to a conventional deadlift. The snatch-grip deadlift is used as a supplementary strength exercise by Olympic weightlifters because it trains the exact bar path and body position mechanics of the snatch's first and second pull phases under supramaximal loads that cannot be used in the full snatch.

name: Overhand Deadlift
technical_cues: Grip the bar with a double overhand grip, no straps
Squeeze the bar as hard as possible before initiating the pull
Pull identically to a conventional deadlift in all other respects
If the grip gives out, terminate the set—do not switch to mixed
Use chalk to maximize friction between skin and bar
biomechanical_why: The double overhand grip places both hands in the same rotational direction, meaning the bar will tend to roll toward the fingers during the pull, which requires maximal activation of the flexor digitorum profundus and flexor digitorum superficialis (origin: medial epicondyle and proximal radius/ulna; insertion: middle and distal phalanges) to maintain contact. This grip is significantly weaker than a mixed or hook grip, making it an excellent grip strength developer as it forces the grip to become the primary training focus and limiting factor, ensuring the finger flexors receive a progressive overload stimulus. Consistently training with double overhand grip builds the forearm flexor hypertrophy and connective tissue strength needed for hook grip tolerance and for strongman events where no alternate grip is available.

name: Reverse Hyper
technical_cues: Lie face down on the reverse hyper pad with hips at the edge
Grip the handles firmly and allow the weight to hang below
Swing the legs up by extending the hips against the load
Hold briefly at the top of the range before lowering
Control the descent to allow traction on the lumbar spine
biomechanical_why: The reverse hyperextension uniquely trains the gluteus maximus and hamstrings in hip extension while simultaneously creating traction on the lumbar spine during the lowering phase, which may reduce intervertebral disc pressure and serve a rehabilitative function in contrast to the compressive forces of squats and deadlifts. The swinging pendulum action of the weight creates a rotational momentum that the erector spinae must control eccentrically during the lowering phase, providing a unique eccentric stimulus to the deep spinal extensors—multifidus (origin: sacrum and mamillary processes; insertion: spinous processes)—at long muscle lengths. The exercise requires no external loading to be effective for lumbar rehabilitation, making it highly scalable, and weighted versions provide progressive overload for the gluteus maximus and hamstrings with minimal spinal compression risk.

name: Glute Drive
technical_cues: Set upper back on a bench with bar or pad over the hips
Plant feet flat on the floor at a comfortable distance from the bench
Drive through both heels to thrust hips to full extension
Squeeze the glutes maximally at the top of each repetition
Lower hips to just above the floor before the next drive
biomechanical_why: The glute drive (or machine hip thrust variation) isolates the gluteus maximus in its most mechanically favorable position for force production—near full hip extension—by using a horizontal loading vector that maintains near-constant tension on the gluteal musculature throughout the range of motion. At full hip extension, the gluteus maximus (origin: posterior sacrum, posterior ilium, and sacrotuberous ligament; insertion: gluteal tuberosity of femur and IT band) operates at close to its optimal sarcomere length for force production, which is a position unavailable during squats where the glutes are most active at deeper knee flexion angles. The stability demands on the hip abductors (gluteus medius and minimus) and adductors during the bilateral drive create co-contraction that reinforces hip joint stability and develops the medial-lateral control important for athletic movements.

name: Zercher Deadlift
technical_cues: Load the bar on pins at a height that allows the elbow crease to reach it
Cradle the bar in both elbow creases before standing
Drive through the legs to stand tall with the bar in the elbows
Keep the elbows tight to the body to prevent bar slip
Lower by hinging back to replace the bar on the pins
biomechanical_why: The Zercher deadlift uses the elbow crease as the contact point for the bar, which means the biceps brachii must work intensively isometrically to prevent the bar from sliding down the forearms while the primary hip and knee extensors perform the lifting work. The anterior load position at the elbow forces the thoracic extensors and core to work much harder than in a conventional deadlift to prevent the torso from collapsing forward, as the moment arm of the barbell relative to the thoracic spine is significantly greater. This unique loading pattern develops positional strength in the upper back and anterior core that transfers directly to atlas stone and sandbag lifting in strongman, where implements must often be carried in a bear-hug position with similar torso demands.
